blocks.body: Verbs + Infinitive collects common verbs that are naturally followed by a to-infinitive.

{"level":"B1","intro":"In this lesson you gather common verbs whose natural pattern is the to-infinitive and learn to recognize these structures quickly in speech and writing.","rules":[{"label":"Ready-made pattern","color":"emerald","text":"It is easier to learn verb + to-infinitive as a whole: want to go, plan to move, promise to help.","example":"They plan to move soon."},{"label":"Intention, decision, promise","color":"blue","text":"Such verbs often talk about intentions, choices, agreements, or willingness to do something.","example":"decide to stay / agree to help"},{"label":"The gerund does not fit everywhere","color":"amber","text":"After many of these verbs, the -ing form either sounds unnatural or gives a different grammar pattern.","example":"hope to see, not hope seeing"}]}

{"title":"1. Common patterns","intro":"Here are a few useful groups of verbs that usually take the to-infinitive.","items":[{"label":"Intention / desire","title":"want / hope / need + to-infinitive","subtitle":"want to leave / hope to win / need to talk"},{"label":"Decision / plan","title":"decide / plan / choose + to-infinitive","subtitle":"decide to stay / plan to travel / choose to wait"},{"label":"Promise / refusal","title":"promise / refuse / agree + to-infinitive","subtitle":"promise to call / refuse to help / agree to join"},{"label":"Attempt / offer","title":"try / offer / learn + to-infinitive","subtitle":"try to finish / offer to carry / learn to drive"}]}

{"title":"2. How these patterns work","sections":[{"label":"Desire, need, intention","color":"emerald","description":"Verbs like want, need, hope often show what a person wants, needs, or expects in a future action.","examples":[{"en":"I want to speak to the manager.","ua":"I want to speak to the manager."},{"en":"We need to leave now.","ua":"We need to leave now."},{"en":"She hopes to find a better solution.","ua":"She hopes to find a better solution."}]},{"label":"Decision, plan, agreement","color":"blue","description":"Verbs such as decide, plan, agree, promise often introduce an action that someone chooses or takes on.","examples":[{"en":"They decided to rent a flat together.","ua":"They decided to rent a flat together."},{"en":"He promised to call after work.","ua":"He promised to call after work."},{"en":"We agreed to meet online.","ua":"We agreed to meet online."}],"note":"In these patterns, the to-infinitive naturally shows the next action or intention."},{"label":"Refusal, attempt, offer","color":"amber","description":"Verbs like refuse, try, offer also often take the to-infinitive when the action is seen as a result or intention.","examples":[{"en":"She refused to sign the paper.","ua":"She refused to sign the paper."},{"en":"Try to stay calm.","ua":"Try to stay calm."},{"en":"I offered to carry the bag.","ua":"I offered to carry the bag."}],"note":"In the lesson about try, remember that try doing can have a different meaning."}]}

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using a gerund after decide.","wrong":"We decided going home.","right":"✅ We decided to go home.","hint":"After decide, the standard pattern is the to-infinitive."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Using a gerund after promise.","wrong":"He promised helping later.","right":"✅ He promised to help later.","hint":"After promise, we talk about an action the person commits to."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Using a gerund after hope in the basic pattern.","wrong":"I hope seeing you next week.","right":"✅ I hope to see you next week.","hint":"In the standard pattern after hope, we use the to-infinitive."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaway","items":["After many verbs, the natural choice is verb + to-infinitive.","This pattern appears especially often after want, need, hope, decide, plan, promise, refuse, agree, offer.","The to-infinitive often points to a future action, an intention, a decision, or an agreement.","If the verb talks about choice, desire, attempt, or promise, first check the pattern with the to-infinitive.","Learn the verb together with the following form, not in isolation."]}
